root.title: Cúram 8.1.2 Getting Started Guide
root.summary: Front matter and overview for the Cúram 8.1.2 Getting Started Guide. It introduces how to begin working with Cúram across the main lifecycle areas: analysis/configuration/customization, business analysis, configuration, development, security, deployment, upgrade, and administration. Key points include the distinction between configuration (no coding) and customization (coding), use of CADT-generated analysis documentation and ERDs for business/technical analysis, role-based configuration administration, development prerequisites such as the Cúram Development Environment and compliant development guidelines, deployment to an application server, upgrade aids like the Cúram Upgrade Pack and UI Upgrade Helper, and initial production administration before go-live. The rest is legal material: notices, privacy policy, and trademarks, including Merative copyright and usage restrictions.

children.title: Note
children.summary: Introductory note for the document. It tells readers to review the “Notices” section on page 29 before using the information or the supported product, and it includes the Merative US L.P. copyright notice (2012, 2024).

children.title: Edition
Edition — pages 5-14
children.summary: Introductory edition/front-matter for Cúram 8.1–8.1.2. Includes copyright/trademark notices and the table of contents. Main visible topics: getting started, analysis/configuration/customization, business analysis, development, security, deployment, upgrade, and administration. It explains that Cúram distinguishes configuration (no coding) from customization (coding), and describes Cúram Analysis Documentation/CADT and ERDs as sources for business/technical analysis.

children.title: Edition — pages 15-24
children.summary: This section introduces key “getting started” topics for Cúram: configuration, development, security, and deployment. It explains that configuration is role-based, development combines configuration/customization and requires the Development Environment plus deployment to an application server, and security guidance should be followed for implementation. It also points to development resources such as API docs, low-level developer docs, bean doc, sample code, and component-specific guidance. Useful for finding setup and foundational implementation information.

children.title: Edition — pages 25-28
children.summary: This section introduces two setup topics: upgrading a custom Cúram application and getting started with administration. It recommends using the Cúram Upgrade Pack, which includes upgrade tools and a guide describing the recommended upgrade process, key migrations, and how to obtain the pack from Cúram Support. It also points to the UI Upgrade Helper for upgrading the Cúram UI from version 7 to version 8. The administration part explains that before go-live you must configure initial production administration and later manage users, organization, and other ongoing system/application changes.

Introductory front matter for Cúram 8.1–8.1.2, including contents and notices. It outlines the main “getting started” areas: analysis/configuration/customization, business analysis, configuration, development, security, deployment, upgrade, and administration. Key facts: configuration is non-coding, customization requires coding, and analysis can use CADT-generated documentation plus ERDs. It also points to development environment setup, compliancy guidance, server/client development resources, security/deployment prerequisites, upgrade tools (Upgrade Pack and UI Upgrade Helper), and initial production administration.

children.title: Notices
Privacy policy
children.summary: This page contains legal and licensing notices, not product guidance: fictitious-name disclaimer, sample code copyright license and “AS IS” warranty disclaimer, and liability limits. It also points to the Merative privacy policy at https://www.merative.com/privacy and lists major trademarks (Merative, IBM, Adobe, Oracle, Linux, Microsoft, UNIX) plus copyright notice © Merative US L.P. 2012, 2024.

children.title: Trademarks
children.summary: States that example names in the document are fictitious and that licensed materials are governed by the Merative Client Agreement. Includes a copyright license for sample source programs, allowing copying/modification/distribution for API-conforming development, with no warranty and no liability. Also provides the Merative privacy policy link. The trademarks list names Merative, IBM, Adobe, Oracle/Java, Linux, Microsoft/Windows, and UNIX as trademarks or registered trademarks, plus a general notice that other names may belong to others.

Legal notices and publication-use terms for Merative documentation. Covers personal and enterprise reproduction limits, prohibition on derivative works or redistribution outside permitted scope, and Merative’s right to withdraw permission. Includes export-law compliance, “as-is” warranty disclaimer, no guarantee of content, no patent license, and disclaimers for third-party products and websites. Ends with copyright © Merative US L.P. 2012, 2024.

children.title: Contents
children.summary: Table of contents for the guide. Lists front matter (Note, Edition, Notices, Privacy policy, Trademarks) and major sections: Getting started; Analysis, configuration, and customization; business analysis; configuration; development (development environment, compliant development, server/client applications, ARM, information resources); security; deployment; upgrade; and administration, with starting page numbers. Useful for navigating the document rather than substantive content.

children.title: 1 Getting started
children.summary: Introduces the “Getting started” section for Cúram. It explains that this area helps users begin with Cúram tasks and features by reviewing high-level tasks and following links to more detailed guidance. Useful as an entry point for newcomers or anyone looking for an overview before diving into specific procedures.

children.title: 2 Analysis, configuration, and customization
children.summary: This section explains how to adapt Cúram’s commercial-off-the-shelf suite to an implementation. It covers the need to analyze business and technical requirements, identify gaps between existing processes and the COTS offering, and decide whether changes can be handled by configuration or require customization. It emphasizes that configuration avoids coding, while customization involves coding, and notes that many needs may be met through configuration alone.

children.title: 3 Getting started with business analysis
children.summary: Explains how to start business analysis for Cúram implementations: define required configuration/customization by reviewing available business documentation and application behavior. Describes Cúram Analysis Documentation and CADT-generated packages, which document metadata and functions from a specific codebase, including views, tables, pages, domains, workflows, rules, evidence, scripts, schemas, and page flows. Notes CADT is provided as-is with no formal support or fixes. Also points to ERDs in PDF under the installation directory.

children.title: 4 Getting started with configuration
children.summary: Explains that during implementation you must configure application options to match requirements, using the configuration documentation to understand available options and requirements. Notes that Cúram provides a role-based administration view for configuration tasks, with system administrators handling technically oriented tasks and general administrators handling user/business-oriented tasks.

children.title: 5 Getting started with development
5.1 Getting started with the Cúram Development Environment
children.summary: Explains that Cúram development requires the Cúram Development Environment, which must be installed to customize applications. Advises developers to install and familiarize themselves with the environment and available documentation before starting. Also notes that Cúram applications must be deployed to an application server to be available for development, and points to related getting-started resources for basic server and client development tasks.

children.title: 5.2 Guidelines for compliant development
children.summary: This section says that when extending Cúram applications, you must follow basic compliancy guidelines to protect custom changes from being overwritten by routine upgrades or fixes. It advises reading the compliancy documentation before starting development.

children.title: 5.3 Getting started with developing server applications
children.summary: Intro to Cúram server application development: use the server developer documentation to learn the standard processes for configuring server applications and customizing server source artifacts. This section is a starting point for basic server-side development tasks.

children.title: 5.4 Getting started with the Cúram Application Reference Model
children.summary: Introduces how to get started with the Cúram Application Reference Model, specifically for understanding how to modify and extend it. Also points to related material on developing client applications, including UI creation, customizing client source artifacts, and client configuration properties such as those in curam-config.xml. The section further directs readers to development information resources, including online resources, API Javadoc in component doc folders, and low-level HTML developer documentation in %CURAM_DIR%\Doc (default C:\Merative\Curam\Development).

children.title: 5.5 Getting started with developing client applications
children.summary: Introduces client application development in Cúram, covering creation of the user interface and customization of client source artifacts. It is a starting point for learning the basic tasks needed to configure and customize Cúram client applications, including client configuration properties such as those in curam-config.xml. This section is useful if you need guidance on client-side setup and customization rather than the Application Reference Model or development resources.

children.title: 5.6 Information resources for development
Information resources online
children.summary: Lists online and local development references for Cúram. In the Cúram Development Environment, it points to API documentation in Javadoc format under each component’s doc subfolder and low-level developer documentation in HTML under %CURAM_DIR%\Doc, viewable via index.html. Useful for developers looking for interfaces, installed-component documentation, and other resources supporting development tasks.

children.title: Information resources in the Cúram Development Environment
API documentation
children.summary: This section points to development information resources in the Cúram Development Environment. It says API documentation is available for each component in Javadoc format under a doc subfolder, and low-level developer documentation is available as HTML in %CURAM_DIR%\Doc. It also notes the default installation path (C:\Merative\Curam\Development) and that index.html opens the documentation. Useful for finding external interfaces and installed-component developer docs.

children.title: Low-level developer documentation
children.summary: This section points developers to the low-level HTML documentation for installed Cúram components. It explains that the docs are located in %CURAM_DIR%\Doc (default C:\Merative\Curam\Development) and can be opened by launching index.html. It also sits within a broader development resources area that includes API/Javadoc documentation, client application development, and information for modifying the Application Reference Model.

children.title: Bean doc
children.summary: This section explains “bean doc,” a generated HML file that documents all available server-side classes and methods for client development. It notes that you can generate bean doc on the client using the build beandoc command before starting development. The section also points to sample code provided under the %CURAM_DIR%\EJBServer\components directory, with component-specific source archives such as the Evidence sample code.

children.title: Sample code
children.summary: Explains where to find component sample code for custom development in Cúram. Sample code is stored under %CURAM_DIR%\EJBServer\components in a component-specific directory, with the default install path noted as C:\Merative\Curam\Development. Gives an example location for the Evidence component: C:\Merative\Curam\Development\EJBServer\components\Evidence\sample\source.zip. Also appears alongside guidance on bean doc generation and other development documentation.

This section lists development resources in the Cúram Development Environment. It covers component API documentation in Javadoc under each component’s doc subfolder, low-level HTML developer docs in %CURAM_DIR%\Doc (default C:\Merative\Curam\Development, opened via index.html), bean doc as generated HML for server-side classes/methods (created with build beandoc), and component sample code under %CURAM_DIR%\EJBServer\components, with an Evidence example path.

This section lists development resources for Cúram. It points to online references and local documentation in the Cúram Development Environment, including component API docs in Javadoc, low-level HTML developer docs in %CURAM_DIR%\Doc, bean doc generated with build beandoc for server-side classes and methods, and sample code under %CURAM_DIR%\EJBServer\components. It also highlights useful docs such as the Data Dictionary, restricted code tables, validation messages, and workflow documentation.

Overview of getting started with Cúram development. It explains that customization requires the Cúram Development Environment, deployment to an application server, and use of the relevant server/client development documentation. It emphasizes compliance guidelines to protect custom changes from upgrades and fixes. The section also introduces starting points for server development, client development, and the Application Reference Model, plus a development resources guide covering online docs, Javadoc, low-level HTML docs in %CURAM_DIR%\Doc, bean doc, sample code, and key references like the Data Dictionary and workflow documentation.

children.title: 6 Getting started with security
children.summary: Introductory guidance for securing a Cúram implementation. It tells readers to review the security documentation to understand required security tasks, and notes that additional component-specific security guidance appears in relevant solution and module documentation. Useful as the starting point for locating overall security setup information.

children.title: 7 Getting started with deployment
children.summary: Explains the basic prerequisite for development: the Cúram application must be deployed to an application server to be available. It also points readers to the “Deploying” section for instructions on deploying Cúram to a development or test environment.

children.title: 8 Getting started with an upgrade
children.summary: Introduces resources for upgrading a custom Cúram application. It highlights the Cúram Upgrade Pack, which includes tools and an upgrade guide covering the recommended upgrade process and significant migrations that may require effort, and the UI Upgrade Helper, used to upgrade the Cúram UI from version 7 to version 8. Notes that the Upgrade Pack is available from Cúram Support and requires access to download.

children.title: 9 Getting started with administration
children.summary: Introduces administration for a production system. It says initial administration settings must be defined and configured before go-live, and ongoing administration covers dynamic system changes such as user updates, organization changes, and other system/application administration tasks. The rest of the pages in this range are legal notices, copyright, privacy policy, and trademarks.
